title: Different Provinces, Same Industrial Policies
gist: Beijing is pushing provinces to tailor industrial policy to local strengths instead of copying trendy sectors like chips, but the latest provincial five-year plans remain nearly identical, suggesting the anti-duplication push is failing because the central government itself keeps directing all provinces toward the same favored industries.

insights.claim: China's central leadership is concerned about waste and duplication caused by local governments pursuing industrial policies that all crowd into the same popular sectors, and is pushing provinces to base policy on local conditions rather than copying trends elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Signals top-level recognition of overcapacity risk in sectors that provinces have piled into simultaneously; watch for future capacity-discipline directives targeting these crowded sectors.
insights.locator: China's leadership is increasingly concerned about waste

insights.claim: Xi Jinping criticized local governments in remarks at the Central Economic Work Conference in December, citing localities all rushing into chip manufacturing as an example of blindly chasing trends instead of pursuing sensible industrial policy.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Top leadership singling out chips as the poster child for wasteful duplication flags semiconductor manufacturing capacity as a sector where Chinese oversupply and price competition risk is already a policy-level concern.
insights.quote: Some localities don’t pay attention to reality and blindly chase trends. They see people going into chips, and they do the same… Why should everyone all choose to do the same job?
insights.locator: Top leader Xi Jinping put it bluntly in remarks

themes.theme: Rhetoric fails to change provincial herding

insights.claim: Despite central admonishments to adapt policy to local competitive advantages, the latest provincial five-year plans show little evidence of differentiation and remain extremely similar to one another across provinces.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Persistent policy homogeneity across provinces implies continued risk of simultaneous capacity build-out and price wars in whichever industries the current five-year plan favors, rather than the intended diversification.
insights.locator: there is not much evidence that the provinces are adapting

insights.claim: The duplication problem is self-reinforcing: because the central government keeps telling local governments which industries to favor, local governments keep concentrating on that same narrow set of politically encouraged industries instead of diversifying.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The stated cause of herding (top-down sector guidance) is the same mechanism Beijing is using to try to fix it, suggesting the anti-duplication campaign is unlikely to succeed until national five-year plan guidance itself becomes less prescriptive.
insights.locator: Because the central government continues to tell local governments

insights.claim: Provincial governments are shifting their focus to a newer set of industries in line with the new national five-year plan, meaning the next wave of local-government-driven capacity buildout will concentrate in whichever sectors that plan designates as priorities.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Investors should track which industries dominate the new national five-year plan's priority lists, since those are the candidates for the next round of province-wide overcapacity and margin compression.
insights.locator: The latest provincial five-year plans show that local authorities
